learningGoal: How to find the difference between neighbouring terms (each number in the pattern)
How to check the difference is constant

screens.title: What's the pattern: 7, 13, 19, 25, 31?
screens.body: Ben counts the number of tiles in each row of a growing pattern: **7, 13, 19, 25, 31**. There's a pattern hiding here! The secret is to look at the **gaps** between the numbers.

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Subtract, check, then confirm the constant difference. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — subtract a pair first, then check another pair, then state the rule.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: What is the constant difference in the sequence **5, 14, 23, 32, 41**?

interaction.question: What is the constant difference?
interaction.correctFeedback: That's right! Each number is **9** more than the last ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Subtract any two neighbours: 14 − 5 = **9**.

screens.title: Finding the difference — the recipe!
screens.body: To find the constant difference in any sequence:

steps.text: Step 1: Subtract each number from the next
Step 2: Check ALL the differences are the same
steps.why: If they're all equal, it's a constant difference

learningGoal: How to avoid miscounting the difference
Why you must check ALL gaps, not just the first one

screens.title: Spot Holly's mistake!
screens.body:
Holly finds the difference in a sequence: **5, 12, 19, 26, 33**.

Holly says: "The difference is **5**." That's not right!

screens.title: Always subtract to check!
screens.body:
Holly counted the gap on her fingers but started counting from 5 instead of 6. The correct difference is 12 − 5 = 7, not 5.

The only way to be sure is to **subtract**: second number minus first number.

screens.title: What's the real difference?
screens.body: What is the **correct** constant difference for **4, 13, 22, 31, 40**?

interaction.question: The constant difference is:
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Subtract: 13 − 4 = **9**. Always subtract to find the difference!

screens.title: Subtract — don't guess!
screens.body: The most common mistakes with differences:

steps.text: Don't confuse the first NUMBER with the difference
steps.why: The first number is where it starts, not how big the jumps are

steps.text: Don't count on fingers from the first number
steps.why: Subtract instead: next number − current number

steps.text: Check at least TWO gaps to be sure
steps.why: If they're both the same, you've got it ✓

subConcepts.name: Continuing a Sequence Using the Rule

learningGoal: How to continue a sequence forwards
How to work backwards to find missing earlier terms

screens.title: What comes next: 4, 11, 18, 25, ...?
screens.body: Grace needs to find the next three terms of a sticker pattern. The sequence so far is **4, 11, 18, 25**. Once you know the rule, finding the next terms is easy!

screens.title: Find the rule, then keep going!
Your turn!
screens.body:
The sequence is **3, 9, 15, 21, ...**
The difference is **+6**. What comes next?

interaction.question: What is the next number after 21?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Add the difference: 21 + 6 = **27**.

screens.title: Continuing sequences — simple!
screens.body: Once you know the constant difference, continuing is easy:

steps.why: Subtract each term from the next one

steps.text: Step 2: Add the difference to the last number
steps.why: This gives you the next number

steps.text: Step 3: Keep going for as many numbers as you need
steps.why: Each new number = previous number + difference ✓

learningGoal: How to work backwards to find earlier terms in a sequence
Why subtracting the difference goes backwards

screens.title: What came BEFORE 19?
screens.body:
Finn knows three terms of a sequence but the first term is hidden: **?, ?, 19, 26, 33**.

We know the sequence goes up by **7** each time. But what if we need to go **backwards**?

interaction.sentence: To go backwards in a sequence, ____ the difference from the first known term
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! Going forward = add, going backward = subtract. 19 − 7 = 12. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — going forward means adding, so going backward means subtracting the difference.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: The sequence is: **?, 24, 33, 42**. The difference is +9. What is the missing number?

interaction.question: What number comes before 24?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! To go backwards, subtract: 24 − 9 = **15**.

screens.title: Forwards and backwards!
screens.body: You can use the constant difference to go in **either direction**:

steps.text: Forward: ADD the difference to the last number
Backward: SUBTRACT the difference from the first number

learningGoal: How to write the nth term rule (a formula that finds any term from its position) for an arithmetic sequence (one that goes up or down by the same amount each time)
How to use the rule to find any term (number in the pattern)

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Difference first, then adjustment, then write the rule. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — find the difference first, then the adjustment (first term minus difference), then write the rule.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: A new sequence: **5, 9, 13, 17, 21**. The difference is **+4** and the rule is **4n + 1**. What is the **8th** term?

interaction.question: The 8th term of the sequence = ?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 4n + 1 when n = 8: 4 × 8 + 1 = **33**.

learningGoal: How to avoid confusing the difference with the first term
Why testing the rule is so important

screens.title: Always test with n = 1!
screens.body:
Holly wrote 5n + 5 because the first term is 5. But when n = 1: 5(1) + 5 = 10, not 5! The first term is already 5 × 1 = 5, so no adjustment is needed.

The correct rule is **5n**.
Test: when n = 1 → **5** ✓

screens.title: Which rule is correct?
screens.body: For the sequence **6, 12, 18, 24**, which is the correct nth term?

interaction.question: The nth term is:
interaction.correctFeedback: Well done! **6n** — always test with n = 1! ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The adjustment is 6 − 6 = 0. The rule is **6n**.

screens.title: The test that catches mistakes!
screens.body: The most common mistake is getting the adjustment wrong. Here's how to avoid it:

steps.text: Find the difference → put it in front of n
Adjustment = first term MINUS the difference
ALWAYS test: does n = 1 give the first term?

learningGoal: How to recognise decreasing sequences where the difference is negative
Why the same method works for both increasing and decreasing

screens.title: This sequence goes DOWN: 40, 34, 28, 22, ...
screens.body: Grace watches a candle getting shorter every hour. The numbers are getting **smaller** — this is a **decreasing** sequence. But the method for finding the rule is exactly the same!

interaction.sentence: In a decreasing sequence, the constant difference is ____
interaction.correctFeedback: Yes! A decreasing sequence has a negative difference — you subtract each time. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — when numbers get smaller, the difference is negative (e.g. -6).

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: The sequence is **45, 37, 29, 21, ...**. The difference is **-8**. What comes next?

interaction.question: What is the next number after 21?
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Subtract 8: 21 − 8 = **13**.

screens.title: Decreasing = negative difference!
screens.body: Decreasing sequences work exactly the same as increasing ones — the difference is just **negative**:

steps.why: Add to get the next number
Subtract to get the next number

steps.text: The method is identical — find difference, then apply it

learningGoal: How to avoid adding when you should subtract
Why the sign of the difference matters

screens.title: Spot Charlie's mistake!
screens.body:
Charlie continues a decreasing sequence: **30, 25, 20, 15, ...**

Charlie says the next number is **20**. That's not right!

screens.title: Subtract, don't add!
screens.body:
Charlie added 5 instead of subtracting! The sequence is DECREASING, so the difference is −5, not +5.

The correct next number: **15 − 5 = 10**

screens.title: What's the correct next number?
screens.body: The sequence is **36, 30, 24, 18, ...**. The difference is **-6**. What really comes next?

interaction.question: The next number is:
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Subtract 6: 18 − 6 = **12**.

screens.title: Check the direction!
screens.body: Before continuing any sequence, check: is it going UP or DOWN?

steps.text: Numbers getting BIGGER → ADD the difference
Numbers getting SMALLER → SUBTRACT the difference

learningGoal: How to spot a sequence that multiplies instead of adds
How to find the multiplier (common ratio)

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: The sequence **5, 10, 20, 40, 80** multiplies by **2** each time. What comes next?

interaction.question: The next number after 80 is:
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Multiply: 80 × 2 = **160**.

screens.title: Multiplying sequences!
screens.body: Not all sequences add or subtract. Some **multiply**:

steps.text: If the differences keep GROWING, try dividing
steps.why: Divide each number by the previous one

steps.text: If the answer is the same each time → geometric!

learningGoal: How to tell the difference between arithmetic (adding the same amount) and geometric (multiplying by the same amount) sequences
When to add and when to multiply

screens.title: Adding vs multiplying!
screens.body:
**Arithmetic** sequences have a constant **difference** (you add the same amount).

**Geometric** sequences have a constant **multiplier** (you multiply by the same amount).

The quick test: check the differences. If they're all the same → arithmetic. If they keep growing → probably geometric!

screens.title: What comes next?
screens.body: The sequence **4, 12, 36, 108** is geometric (×3). What is the next number?

interaction.question: The next number is:
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Multiply: 108 × 3 = **324**.

screens.title: Arithmetic vs geometric — know the difference!
screens.body: Two types of sequence you'll see in the 11+:

steps.text: Quick test: check if differences are constant
steps.why: If not, try dividing each number by the previous one ✓

learningGoal: How to recognise square, triangular and cube numbers
Why they are called 'special' sequences

screens.title: A special sequence: 1, 4, 9, 16, 25, ...
screens.body: Grace arranges dots in square patterns and gets the numbers: **1, 4, 9, 16, 25, 36**. These aren't random — they're **square numbers**! They pop up everywhere in maths.

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: What is the **10th square number**?

interaction.question: The 10th square number is:
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The answer is **100**.

screens.title: The three special sequences to remember!

learningGoal: How to quickly identify whether a number is square, triangular or cube
Why learning these by heart speeds up the 11+ exam

screens.title: Is 36 a special number?
screens.body:
Oscar needs to recognise special numbers in a test. The number is **36**. Let's check each type:

- **Square?** Can you find a number that times itself gives 36?
- **Cube?** Can you find a number that times itself three times gives 36?
- **Triangular?** Is it in the sequence 1, 3, 6, 10, 15, 21, 28, 36...?

steps.text: Cube? No whole number × itself × itself = 36

screens.title: Learn them by heart!
screens.body:
36 is BOTH a square number (6×6) AND a triangular number (1+2+3+4+5+6+7+8)

The fastest way to spot these in the exam is to **memorise** them. Here are the ones you need:

screens.title: Quick — what type is it?
screens.body: Is **64** a square number, a cube number, or both?

interaction.question: 64 is:
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! 64 = 8×8 (square) and 4×4×4 (cube).

steps.why: Think: can you make it by multiplying a number by itself?

screens.title: Spot special numbers instantly!
screens.body: In the 11+ exam, recognising special numbers quickly saves precious time:

steps.why: These appear in MANY question types

learningGoal: How to spot a sequence where each number is the sum of the two before it
How this pattern appears in nature

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: In the sequence **2, 5, 7, 12, 19, ...**, each number is the sum of the two before it. What comes next?

interaction.question: The next number is:
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Add the last two: 12 + 19 = **31**.

steps.text: Rule: each number = sum of the previous two

screens.title: The Fibonacci rule!
screens.body: In a Fibonacci-style sequence, each number is the **sum of the two before it**:

steps.text: Check: does each number = the two before it added together?
To find the next number: add the last two numbers
This pattern appears in sunflowers, pinecones and shells!

learningGoal: How to find missing terms in a Fibonacci-style sequence (where each number is the sum of the two before it)
How to work backwards in a Fibonacci sequence

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: Find the missing number: **1, 4, 5, ?, 14**

interaction.question: The missing number is:
interaction.correctFeedback: Spot on! The missing number is **9**. Check: 5 + 9 = 14 ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! The missing number is **9**. Check: 5 + 9 = 14.

steps.text: Rule: each number = sum of the two before
steps.why: Use the numbers around the gap

screens.title: Finding gaps in Fibonacci!
screens.body: When a Fibonacci-style sequence has a missing number:

steps.text: If you know a number and the sum → subtract to find the other
If you know the two before → add them
Always check: does the answer work with its neighbours?
steps.why: Both sides of the gap should check out ✓

learningGoal: How to spot a sequence that uses multiply then add (or subtract)
How to work out a two-step rule

interaction.correctFeedback: Perfect order! Take the last number, multiply, then add. ✓
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite — take the last number, multiply first, then add. The order matters!

screens.title: Your turn!
screens.body: The rule is **×2 then +1**. The sequence is **1, 3, 7, 15, 31, ...**. What comes next?

interaction.question: The next number is:
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Apply the rule: 31×2+1=63.

screens.title: Two-step rules — how to crack them!
screens.body: When a sequence doesn't have a constant difference or constant multiplier, try a **two-step rule**:

steps.text: Try: multiply by 2 then add something
Try: multiply by 3 then add something
Test your rule on ALL the numbers
steps.why: If it works for every pair → you've cracked it! ✓

learningGoal: How to avoid applying operations in the wrong order
Why the order of multiply and add matters

screens.title: Order matters!
screens.body:
Charlie did +1 THEN ×2 instead of ×2 THEN +1. The order matters!.

The correct answer: **31 × 2 + 1 = 63**

screens.title: What's the correct next number?
screens.body: The sequence is **1, 5, 13, 29, ...**. The rule is **×2 then +3**. What comes next?

interaction.question: The next number is:
interaction.incorrectFeedback: Not quite! Apply the rule in order: 29 × 2 + 3 = 61.

screens.title: Multiply FIRST, then add!
screens.body: In two-step rules, the **order** of operations matters:

steps.text: ×2 then +1 means: multiply by 2 FIRST, then add 1
Always do the multiply/divide BEFORE the add/subtract
steps.why: Unless the question specifically says otherwise ✓
